questions.scenario: A pipeline says the deploy succeeded, but the portal page Maya is viewing does not show the new resource. The deployment log says `/subscriptions/sub-dev-001/resourceGroups/rg-devpolaris-orders-dev`, while the portal header says `sub-prod-001`. What should she conclude first?

options.text: The resource may exist in a different subscription or resource group than the one she is viewing, so she should verify the deployment scope before redeploying.
The deployment may have targeted the intended subscription, but the portal search index has not refreshed yet, so wait before checking scope.
The app image failed to start, so the missing portal resource is a runtime health issue.
The resource group name is enough evidence, so the subscription mismatch can be ignored.

questions.explanation: The evidence shows a scope mismatch. Before creating duplicates or touching production, verify tenant, subscription, resource group, and resource ID. Runtime health is a later question after the resource is found.

questions.scenario: `devpolaris-orders-api` starts, but every checkout fails with `403 Forbidden` when reading Key Vault. The same secret name works in staging. What should the team check first?

options.text: Which managed identity the production app is using and whether that identity has the required Key Vault access.
Whether the Node.js package manager cached an old dependency during build.
Whether the public DNS record has a shorter TTL than the staging record.
Whether the storage account has zone redundancy enabled.

questions.explanation: A Key Vault 403 is identity and permission evidence. Build cache, DNS TTL, and storage redundancy can cause real problems, but they do not explain a secret-read authorization failure.

questions.scenario: A background export feature works during a smoke test, but exported files disappear after the Container Apps revision restarts. The code wrote files to `/tmp/exports` inside the container. Which mistake is most likely?

options.text: The app stored durable business output in runtime-local storage instead of a storage service such as Blob Storage.
The app used too many tags, so Azure deleted untagged files during restart.
The region was too close to users, so restart behavior changed.
The export worker may be writing to a mounted path whose backing storage was never configured in production.

questions.explanation: Container-local files are tied to the running copy and can disappear when the copy is replaced. Durable exports need a storage service. Tags, region distance, and billing ownership do not make local runtime files durable.

questions.scenario: A teammate says, `Azure SQL is managed, so we do not need to plan anything else.` The production checklist still has open items for access, backups, restore testing, and migrations. Which responsibilities still belong to the team?

options.text: Choosing the right database, region, firewall or private network path, and access model.
Planning backup retention, restore testing, and what the app should do during an outage.
Writing application queries and migrations that keep order data correct.
Replacing failed Microsoft datacenter hardware by hand.

questions.explanation: Managed services reduce infrastructure work, but they do not remove application, configuration, access, backup, and recovery responsibilities. Replacing physical hardware is the provider side of the line.

questions.scenario: The API can resolve the Azure SQL hostname, but connections time out only from production. The app identity is correct and the database exists. Which foundation-level area should the team inspect next?

options.text: The network path and database access rules, such as private endpoint, firewall rules, subnet routing, or allowed Azure services settings.
The article title in the roadmap, because timeouts usually mean documentation metadata is stale.
The public endpoint path, because the frontend and backend may still be routed through different network paths.
The release artifact, because staging and production may run different image tags even when the code appears similar.

questions.explanation: A timeout after name resolution often points to network reachability or firewall rules. Identity is a separate check and has already been cleared in the scenario. The other options ignore the evidence.
